THE LAW
OFFICES OF
ROBERT
J LOGAN
152 NORTH THIRD STREET
SUITE 201 • SAN JOSE, CA 95112
TELEPHONE • 408.287.2156
FACSIMILE • 408.280.1749

FPPC

AUG 1 4 1992

August 6, 1992

Mr. John Wallis
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice (Gov. Code § 83114[b])

Dear Mr. Wallis:

The attached report from Appraisal Research Corporation ("ARC") delineates
the factual setting surrounding this request for advice pertaining to the Sky Park
Project in the City of Scotts Valley and participation in the decision making by
Robert Hanna. The factual background is also applicable to Planning Commissioners
Michael Shulman and Jackie Heald who also have their principal residence within
2500 feet of the boundary of the Project. The appraisal analysis prepared by ARC
to determine the affect on Mr. Hanna's property would be equally applicable in
determining the affect on Mr. Shulman's and Ms. Heald's property. Mr. Shulman has
already sent you a prior letter, designated 92-439 by your office, which was assigned
to Jill Stecher.

The City of Scotts Valley has a population of approximately 9,000 persons.
Therefore, the materiality rule set out in Section 18701.3 of the FPPC regulations for
properties owned by affected individuals within 2500 feet of the boundaries of the
project affects a large number of residents. In the case of this particular project,
mapping out an area of 2500 feet from the project boundaries includes approximately
40% of the total residences in the City of Scotts Valley. The impacts of the project
would, therefore, appear to affect the individuals seeking this opinion in the same
fashion as a significant part of the public generally. (See FPPC Regulation §
18702[c]) It is my opinion that the exception regarding affect on the public generally
is applicable in this case.

None of the affected individuals reside within 300 feet of the project area. All
of the individuals live in an area more than 300 but less than 2500 feet away. The
ARC analysis appears to support the conclusion that the impact on Mr. Hanna, and
by extension Mr. Shulman and Ms. Heald are not materially affected as defined in
Section 18702.3(a)(3).

Wallis/FPPC
August 10, 1992
Page 2

It is requested that the FPPC review this matter and, hopefully, concur in the
above conclusion. We are seeking an expedited opinion because we believe the issue
relatively simple yet my clients would like the protection afforded by Section 83114.
A prompt response would be greatly appreciated as the planning process for Sky Park
is about to commence.

Sincerely yours,

Robert J. Logan
City Attorney

RJL/kl
encls
cc:    Hanna
       Heald
       Schulman

92-555

ARC
APPRAISAL RESEARCH CORPORATION
APPRAISALS CONSULTATION RIGHT OF WAY REAL ESTATE ECONOMICS
1828 THE ALAMEDA • SAN JOSE, CA 95126 • PHONE 408-247-4371 • FAX 408-984-8782

August 4, 1992

Mr. Robert Logan, City Attorney
City of Scotts Valley
One Civic Center Drive
Scotts Valley, CA 95066

Analysis of Potential Conflict of Interest; Mr. Robert Hanna & Skypark

Dear Mr. Logan;

At the request of Mr. Robert Hanna, Planning Director of Scotts Valley, to investigate and
analyze the potential for a conflict of interest with respect to his decision making capacity relating to
the Skypark planning process.

Background

As part of the implementation of the Political Reform Act, passed as Proposition 9 in 1974,
the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) has developed rules and regulations governing
potential conflict of interest situations for government officials, both elected and appointed. In
general terms, a conflict of interest is present if all the following occur: 1) the official makes,
participates in, or uses his or her official position to influence a public decision; 2) it is foreseeable
that the decision will affect the official's economic interest; 3) the effect of the decision on the
official's economic interest will be material; and 4) the effect of the decision on the official's
economic interest will be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

Currently, the City is in the process of adopting a Specific Plan for a 98 acre site known as
Skypark. If the area were to develop in accordance with the permitted uses of the Specific Plan, as
much as 200 to 350 new housing units, 400,000 to 500,000 square feet of retail and office, a new
school and 21 acres of open space could result. For a city the size of Scotts Valley, this is a
significant development. At this time, there is no certainty that this development will occur as there
are many obstacles to overcome or variables that affect development such as availability of
financing, market conditions and overall economic conditions. However, for purposes of this
analysis, development in the near to mid-term time frame, ie. five to ten years, will be assumed.

Mr. Hanna owns a single family house, located at 110 Viki Court in which he has an interest
of over $1,000 and the house is located approximately 1,500 to 1,600 feet from the nearest project
boundary. These two factors, with others, trigger the need to investigate a potential conflict of
interest.

SAN FRANCISCO • SAN JOSE • REDWOOD CITY

AUG-05-'92 13:50 ID: TEL NO:4085946782 H15B P03

page 2
Conflict Analysis: Hanna & Skypark
August 4, 1992

Analysis

With respect to component 1) as listed above, Mr. Hanna, as Planning Director of the city is responsible for preparing plans and recommendations for consideration by the planning commission and the city council. In addition, he reviews specific project proposals and approves or disapproves or makes recommendations for approval or disapproval. He is in a position to influence public decisions, even though he does not make the decision itself. Therefore, this criteria is met.

Component 2) seeks to establish whether it is foreseeable that a decision will affect the official's economic interest. Mr. Hanna's economic interest is established by an equity in his house over $1,000. As stated above, the actual development of Skypark is not certain and is dependent on many factors and decisions well beyond the control of Mr. Hanna. However, given the desirability of Scotts Valley, the past growth it has experienced and that it also provides retail, employment and services for surrounding smaller communities, it is foreseeable that Skypark will develop in the next five to ten years. This component is also met.

A further definition of component 3) is that the effect is considered material if an official's real property is within a zone between 300 and 2,500 feet of a project boundary and the fair market value of the property is increased or decreased by more than $10,000 as a result of the project. Mr. Hanna's house is within this zone. The effect of this project on the fair market value of a single family house in five to ten years is not as easily determined. Factors that affect a change in value of a single family house are many and complex. Key factors that affect change include increase or decrease in employment opportunities, balance or imbalance of supply and demand, availability and terms of financing, direct proximity to new uses either beneficial or detrimental, changes in traffic, desirability of the subject property as compared to competing properties, decline or upgrading of the neighborhood, overall economic conditions both local and national, and more or less restrictive land use regulations. The project will in no way enhance or diminish Mr. Hanna's use of his house but Skypark could contribute incrementally to some of the other factors but the contributions could be both positive and negative. For example, new employment could increase housing demand and possibly higher prices but could also increase traffic congestion which may depress prices. Also, increased housing demand may be totally offset by the proposed new housing which is part of the project.

In a recent study, prepared by Appraisal Research Corporation, where data was available, the effect of a regional shopping center on house values of an abutting neighborhood was analyzed. This was done by comparing long term appreciation of two neighborhoods, one directly adjacent to the new development and the other some distance away on the other side of a major arterial. In addition, city wide data was also analyzed. The time period encompassed the completion of the development. Within the statistical accuracy of the available data, there was no discernable difference between the rate of appreciation of all three areas.

page 3
Conflict Analysis: Hanna & Skypark
August 4, 1992

An effort was made to perform a similar analysis in Scotts Valley but there is insufficient
readily available data to provide any degree of statistical reliability. Further work to attempt to
collect the needed data is beyond the scope of the assignment and, based on the size of Scotts Valley,
there is reason to suspect that even with an indepth search, the data uncovered would not be
statistically valid.

In addition, a limited search of appraisal literature was made to identify any relevant
research relating to the subject matter. None was found.

In the absence of hard data, it is my opinion that the planning and ultimate construction of
the Skypark development will not materially affect the value of Mr. Hanna's house, or that any
effect will be either significantly below $10,000 or non-existent. Therefore, the potential for conflict
of interest as established by component 3) is not met.

Component 4) deals with the issue whether the decision will be distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, which includes a significant portion of the public. When a zone of effect of
2,500 feet from the projects boundaries is plotted, it includes most of the land zoned for higher
density within the city limits and contains a significant portion, estimated at 41%, of the total
number of housing units in the city. Therefore, any effect of the project on house values would be
area-wide in nature and any effect on a change of value of Mr. Hanna's house would be
indistinguishable from a significant portion of the public generally. The criteria for component 4) is
not met.

In summary, by the nature of Mr. Hanna's position and the size and scope of the Skypark
project, the criteria of components 1) and 2) are met. With respect to whether the effect of the
project on the value of Mr. Hanna's house would be material, there is no sound methodology to
make a quantitative determination, which could raise questions as to this finding. The clear finding
that any potential effect would not be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally
indicates that Mr. Hanna is not in a position of conflict of interest with respect to any of his official
actions relating to the proposed Skypark project.

Should you have any questions, please feel free to contact me.

Sincerely;

APPRAISAL RESEARCH CORPORATION

John Norberg

cc Mr. Robert Hanna, Planning Director
Mr. Charles Comstock, City Manager

SENT BY:Xerox Telecopier 7021 : 8-7-92 : 11:00 :        4082863256-                2901749!# 1


                           California
                           Fair Political
                           Practices Commission


                                        August 4, 1992


Michael Shulman
111 Bean Creek Road, #80
Scotts Valley, CA 95066

                                    Re: Letter No. 92-439

Dear Mr. Shulman:

       Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on August 3, 1992 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact Jill Stecher, an attorney in the Legal Division,
directly at (916) 322-5601.

       We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the
we will answer it as quickly as we can.     (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

       You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

SH:aa



428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95812 ● (916) 322-5660

SENT BY:Xerox Telecopier 7021 : 8-6-92 ; 8:58 ;        4082963256-        2801/49:# 2

                                        Michael Shulman
                                        111 Bean Creek Road, #80
                                        Scotts Valley, CA  95066


        Fair Political Practices Commission
        428 J Street, Suite 800
        PO Box 807
        Sacramento, CA  95804

        Subject: Request for written ruling

        Reference: Potential Conflict of Interest for Public Official

        I am a Planning Commissioner for the City of Scotts Valley, in
        Santa Cruz County. There is a major project before us that my
        personal residence is within 2500 feet of. Our city attorney
        suggested that a written ruling from the FPPC would be prudent,
        due to the potential for material affect.

        I believe that I have no conflict based on the requirement that
        any effect on my economic interest will not be distinguishable
        from the effect on the general public. This belief is based on
        the following criteria:

        1.     The population of Scotts Valley is approx. 9,000, with city
               limits comprising approx. 4 square miles. We thus fit the
               criteria for a small Jurisdiction.
        2.     Planning Commissioners are required to reside within the
               Jurisdiction.
        3.     All city elections are at-large.
        4.     My residence (a condominium) is approx. 1000 feet from the
               nearest border of the project; there is no foreseeable
               direct effect of the project on my residence.

        Based on the above, your booklet on the Conflict of Interest Law
        (under the section entitled "Public Generally Rule for Small
        Jurisdictions") clears me to participate in the closed sessions on this
        project. However, since the booklet is not the law, per se, I am
        requesting a written ruling from you for the record.

        If there is any other information you need to complete this
        request, please call me at (408) 985-2400, ext. 2770. Additional
        information on the project under discussion can be obtained from
        the City Planning Department Secretary, Ms. Sandy Adams, at (408)
        438-2324.

        Your prompt response would be most appreciated, as the next
        hearing on this matter is scheduled for August 26.

        Sincerely


        Michael Shulman

July 31, 1992


Mr. John Norberg
Appraisal Research Corporation
1929 The Alameda
San Jose, CA. 95126

RE: "Letter of Opinion" on Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Norberg:

We have discussed the Fair Political Practices Commission's
requirements regarding a conflict of interest. I have enclosed a
copy of "A Guide to the Political Reform Act of 1974" from the
California Fair Political Practices Commission. The particular
situation that we have discussed relates to the development of
the "Skypark" property in the City of Scotts Valley. The facts
are that I am the Planning Director for the City of Scotts
Valley. The City is considering a Specific Plan to develop the
Skypark property consisting of approximately 93 acres. The
Development would include 21 acres of Public Use/Open Space,
between 200 and 350 residential units and 400,000 to 500,000
square feet of commercial uses (retail and office).

You have a copy of the Specific Plan that was prepared in June of
1992 and I have included a summary of the project description
from the Environmental Impact Report that was prepared. My
residence is located approximately 1,200 to 1,300 feet from the
exterior boundaries of the proposed development. I have included
a map showing the relationship of the development to my
residence.

You've indicated that you have prepared "Letters of Opinion" as
to the effect of developments on Single Family Dwellings as part
of your appraisal work. The task in this particular instance is
to determine whether the proposed development would increase or
decrease the fair market value of my residence by $10,000 (see
page 15 of the Guide to the Political Reform Act of 1974).

THE LAW
OFFICES OF
ROBERT
J LOGAN

152 NORTH THIRD STREET
SUITE 201 · SAN JOSE, CA 95112
TELEPHONE · 408.287.2156
FACSIMILE · 408.280.1749

August 6, 1992

Mr. John Wallis
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Advice (Gov. Code § 83114[b])

Dear Mr. Wallis:

The attached report from Appraisal Research Corporation ("ARC") delineates
the factual setting surrounding this request for advice pertaining to the Sky Park
Project in the City of Scotts Valley and participation in the decision making by
Robert Hanna. The factual background is also applicable to Planning Commissioners
Michael Shulman and Jackie Heald who also have their principal residence within
2500 feet of the boundary of the Project. The appraisal analysis prepared by ARC
to determine the affect on Mr. Hanna's property would be equally applicable in
determining the affect on Mr. Shulman's and Ms. Heald's property. Mr. Shulman has
already sent you a prior letter, designated 92-439 by your office, which was assigned
to Jill Stecher.

The City of Scotts Valley has a population of approximately 9,000 persons.
Therefore, the materiality rule set out in Section 18701.3 of the FPPC regulations for
properties owned by affected individuals within 2500 feet of the boundaries of the
project affects a large number of residents. In the case of this particular project,
mapping out an area of 2500 feet from the project boundaries includes
approximately 40% of the total residences in the City of Scotts Valley. The impacts of the project
would, therefore, appear to affect the individuals seeking this opinion in the same
fashion as it is a significant part of the public generally. (See FPPC Regulation §
18702[c]) It is my opinion that the exception regarding affect on the public generally
is applicable in this case.

None of the affected individuals reside within 300 feet of the project area. All
of the individuals live in an area more than 300 but less than 2500 feet away. The
ARC analysis appears to support the conclusion that the impact on Mr. Hanna, and
by extension Mr. Shulman and Ms. Heald are not materially affected as defined in
Section 18702.3(a)(3).

Wallis/FPPC
August 10, 1992
Page 2

It is requested that the FPPC review this matter and, hopefully, concur in the
above conclusion. We are seeking an expedited opinion because we believe the issue
relatively simple yet my clients would like the protection afforded by Section 83114.
A prompt response would be greatly appreciated as the planning process for Sky Park
is about to commence.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
Robert J. Logan
City Attorney

sections.facts:
As part of the implementation of the Political Reform Act, passed as Proposition 9 in 1974, the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC) has developed rules and regulations governing potential conflict of interest situations for government officials, both elected and appointed. In general terms, a conflict of interest is present if all the following occur: 1) the official makes, participates in, or uses his or her official position to influence a public decision; 2) it is foreseeable that the decision will affect the official's economic interest; 3) the effect of the decision on the official's economic interest will be material; and 4) the effect of the decision on the official's economic interest will be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

Currently, the City is in the process of adopting a Specific Plan for a 98 acre site known as Skypark. If the area were to develop in accordance with the permitted uses of the Specific Plan, as much as 200 to 350 new housing units, 400,000 to 500,000 square feet of retail and office, a new school and 21 acres of open space could result. For a city the size of Scotts Valley, this is a significant development. At this time, there is no certainty that this development will occur as there are many obstacles to overcome or variables that affect development such as availability of financing, market conditions and overall economic conditions. However, for purposes of this analysis, development in the near to mid-term time frame, ie. five to ten years, will be assumed.

Mr. Hanna owns a single family house, located at 110 Viki Court in which he has an interest of over $1,000 and the house is located approximately 1,500 to 1,600 feet from the nearest project boundary. These two factors, with others, trigger the need to investigate a potential conflict of interest.
Conflict Analysis: Hanna & Skypark
August 4, 1992
sections.analysis:
With respect to component 1) as listed above, Mr. Hanna, as Planning Director of the city is responsible for preparing plans and recommendations for consideration by the planning commission and the city council. In addition, he reviews specific project proposals and approves or disapproves or makes recommendations for approval or disapproval. He is in a position to influence public decisions, even though he does not make the decision itself. Therefore, this criteria is met.

Component 2) seeks to establish whether it is foreseeable that a decision will affect the official's economic interest. Mr. Hanna's economic interest is established by an equity in his house over $1,000. As stated above, the actual development of Skypark is not certain and is dependant on many factors and decisions well beyond the control of Mr. Hanna. However, given the desirability of Scotts Valley, the past growth it has experienced and that it also provides retail, employment and services for surrounding smaller communities, it is foreseeable that Skypark will develop in the next five to ten years. This component is also met.

A further definition of component 3) is that the effect is considered material if an official's real property is within a zone between 300 and 2,500 feet of a project boundary and the fair market value of the property is increased or decreased by more than $10,000 as a result of the project. Mr. Hanna's house is within this zone. The effect of this project on the fair market value of a single family house in five to ten years is not as easily determined. Factors that affect a change in value of a single family house are many and complex. Key factors that affect change include increase or decrease in employment opportunities, balance or imbalance of supply and demand, availability and terms of financing, direct proximity to new uses either beneficial or detrimental, changes in traffic, desirability of the subject property as compared to competing properties, decline or upgrading of the neighborhood, overall economic conditions both local and national, and more or less restrictive land use regulations. The project will in no way enhance or diminish Mr. Hanna's use of his house but Skypark could contribute incrementally to some of the other factors but the contributions could be both positive and negative. For example, new employment could increase housing demand and possibly higher prices but could also increase traffic congestion which may depress prices. Also, increased housing demand may be totally offset by the proposed new housing which is part of the project.

In a recent study, prepared by Appraisal Research Corporation, where data was available, the effect of a regional shopping center on house values of an abutting neighborhood was analyzed. This was done by comparing long term appreciation of two neighborhoods, one directly adjacent to the new development and the other some distance away on the other side of a major arterial. In addition, city wide data was also analyzed. The time period encompassed the completion of the development. Within the statistical accuracy of the available data, there was no discernable difference between the rate of appreciation of all three areas.
Conflict Analysis: Hanna & Skypark
August 4, 1992

An effort was made to perform a similar analysis in Scotts Valley but there is insufficient readily available data to provide any degree of statistical reliability. Further work to attempt to collect the needed data is beyond the scope of the assignment and, based on the size of Scotts Valley, there is reason to suspect that even with an indepth search, the data uncovered would not be statistically valid.

In addition, a limited search of appraisal literature was made to identify any relevant research relating to the subject matter. None was found.

In the absence of hard data, it is my opinion that the planning and ultimate construction of the Skypark development will not materially affect the value of Mr. Hanna's house, or that any effect will be either significantly below $10,000 or non-existent. Therefore, the potential for conflict of interest as established by component 3) is not met.

Component 4) deals with the issue whether the decision will be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, which includes a significant portion of the public. When a zone of effect of 2,500 feet from the projects boundaries is plotted, it includes most of the land zoned for higher density within the city limits and contains a significant portion, estimated at 41%, of the total number of housing units in the city. Therefore, any effect of the project on house values would be area-wide in nature and any effect on a change of value of Mr. Hanna's house would be indistinguishable from a significant portion of the public generally. The criteria for component 4) is not met.

In summary, by the nature of Mr. Hanna's position and the size and scope of the Skypark project, the criteria of components 1) and 2) are met. With respect to whether the effect of the project on the value of Mr. Hanna's house would be material, there is no sound methodology to make a quantitative determination, which could raise questions as to this finding. The clear finding that any potential effect would not be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally indicates that Mr. Hanna is not in a position of conflict of interest with respect to any of his official actions relating to the proposed Skypark project.

The ARC analysis appears to support the conclusion that the impact on Mr. Hanna, and by extension Mr. Shulman and Ms. Heald are not materially affected as defined in Section 18702.3(a)(3). Wallis/FPPC August 10, 1992 Page 2 It is requested that the FPPC review this matter and, hopefully, concur in the above conclusion. We are seeking an expedited opinion because we believe the issue relatively simple yet my clients would like the protection afforded by Section 83114. A prompt response would be greatly appreciated as the planning process for Sky Park is about to commence. Sincerely yours, Robert J.
